VIII

It was a delightful day, warm, yet bracing: a day on which the peasant, after a hearty sleep, jumps up as he wakes, andвБ†вАФhis prayers first saidвБ†вАФsets to work with scarce the shadow of a yawn.

A huge red globe was slowly ascending the sky, on whose immeasurable expanse, amongst a few thin filaments of haze, there floated cluster on cluster of woolly clouds.

The breeze bustled about, with the air of a farmer calling his household up at dawn; rousing the limp corn, blowing away and dispersing the mists, tossing about the overhanging branches, rushing round the orchards and dashing in and scattering the last cherry-blossoms like snow upon the ground.

Lipka too was waking and getting up swiftly. Many a dishevelled head peeped out upon the world with drowsy eyes: some were washing; not a few half-dressed women carried water to their houses. Here a man was splitting logs; there carts were being wheeled out into the roadway. Smoke rose in festoons from the chimneys, and lie-abeds were rated by shrill tongues.

Early it was. The eastern sun was not more than a manвАЩs height in the sky, and shooting its ruddy rays aslant through the orchard-trees; yet everybody was already in lively motion.

The wind had fled away somewhere; they enjoyed a pleasant calm, a fresh balmy morning: the sun played upon the waters, from every roof the dew dripped in pearly drops, swallows swept through the clear air, storks seeking a regale flapped away from their nests. Chanticleer beat his wings and crowed lustily on the hedges, and cackling geese led their fledgelings down to the rose-red pond. In the cowsheds the cattle lowed: around them and about the yards, cows were being hurriedly milked. From every enclosure they were driving oxen into the roads, and they lumbered along, bellowing lazily, and rubbing themselves against trees and fences; while sheep that went by, lifting their heads and bleating, flocked to the middle of the ways amid clouds of dust. All these were hurried to the open space in front of the church, where elderly peasants on horseback, cracking whips and swearing to the top of their bent, assembled the scattering droves and flocks, and urged the laggards forward.

Somewhat after these came the gooseherds, driving their snowy gaggling bands, or one leading a cow, or taking out a hobbled horse to feed in the fallows.

All these, however, had soon passed out, and the rest of the villagers were making ready to go to the fair; this was about a week after the menвАЩs return from prison. Everything in Lipka had little by little returned more or less to its usual state.

Not that all was yet as it should be. They were still rather indolent, often lying too long abed. Some paid too frequent visits to the tavernвБ†вАФto be abreast of the news, they said; and not a few would squander half a day going about and gossiping: others would scamp their most pressing work. To get into gear again, when once out, was no easy matter, after such a spell of forced inactivity. But things were improving daily: the tavern had fewer and fewer guests on workdays, for want had caught men by the throats, and was forcing them to labour in the sweat of their brows.

All the same, as that day there was a fair in Tymov, they preferred to go there and put the work off.

Besides, the days of dearth before harvest-time had begun early and with such severity that a bitter cry now arose in most huts. Whatever, then, they still had to sell, they took in all haste to the fair. And others also went, but only to chat with their neighbours and have a look round, and perhaps drink a nip of vodka.

Everyone had his own troubles; and where should people find comfort, or pour out their complaints, or seek words of good advice, unless either at the fair, or at the local festival?

So, no sooner had the cattle been all driven out to graze, those that had carts got them ready, and those that had none started off on foot.

The poorest were first on the road. Filipka, to her sorrow, drove six old geese before her; she had to sacrifice them. Her husband had fallen sick on returning, and she had nothing to put in the pot.

Some komorniki, too, were taking heifers that were but recently with calf. Misery has long legs and sharp claws; and Gregory the Wrymouth had to sell a milch-cow, though he possessed eight acres; while his neighbour Joseph Vahnik was driving a sow with all her farrow.

They had to keep things going as best they could. More than one was so hard up that he was forced to sell his best horse. For instance, Gulbas. He owed Balcerkova fifteen roubles; she had brought an action and obtained a sentence against him. So, amid the tears of his family, the poor wretch went off astride of his chestnut mare to sell it.

Wagon after wagon rolled on close together. Well-to-do farmers were also taking some of their possessions there: they had to pay their taxes, as the Voyt reminded them. Many goodwives likewise were carrying things to the fair: hens clucked under their aprons in carts; and those on foot had eggs or butter in their kerchiefs. Some bore on their shoulders articles of holiday attire, or pieces of linen for sale.

Mass had been said earlier than usual, and more hastily too; and Teresa the soldierвАЩs wife, who had to speak with the priest, came just as he was leaving church for his breakfast. She durst not accost him then, and stood waiting outside the garden palings for him to come out; but before she could reach him, he had got into the britzka and driven away to Tymov.

She sighed, looking sorrowfully after him, as he went up the poplar road, whence a cloud of dust continually arose, to settle down in the fields around: the carts clattered on as before, and a thin line of red petticoats, in Indian file by the roadside, twinkled now and again among the trees. Presently Lipka relapsed into silence. The mill, the smithy, were closed; shortly the roads were also deserted, and all that remained at home were busy at work in the gardens, or pottering about the enclosures.

Teresa went home in sore trouble.

She lived beyond the church, close to Matthew, in a bit of a hut consisting of one large room and the half of a passage! At the division of the property, her brother had halved the cabin, and carried his share away to rebuild it on his own land. The sawn timbers of the roof and walls stood out like gaunt ribs against the soot-begrimed chimney.

Nastka saw her from her threshold, there being but a narrow strip of orchard between them.

вАЬWell? well? has he read your letter?вАЭ she cried, running to her.

Teresa explained her disappointment.

вАЬI fancy the organist is able to read writing. He could make it out.вАЭ

вАЬCertainly; but how can I go empty-handed?вАЭ

вАЬTake him a few eggs.вАЭ

вАЬHere I have ducksвАЩ eggs only; Mother has taken the others to sell.вАЭ

вАЬNo matter; he wonвАЩt refuse ducksвАЩ eggs.вАЭ

вАЬI would fain go; but I fear so much! If I but knew what is written there!вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She took from her bosom a letter of her husband, which the Voyt had brought her from the office the day before. вАЬWhat can there be in that letter?вАЭ

Nastka took the grimy paper from her hands and, sitting down on the step of the stile, while Teresa seated herself on the upper bar, made a painful attempt to read it. Teresa, with her hands on her chin, gazed in terror at the cabalistic signs that Nastka was trying to spell out. But she could make out no more than вАЬPraised be Jesus Christ!вАЭ at the beginning.

вАЬI cannot read any farther: вАЩtis useless. But Matthew would manage, no doubt.вАЭ

She flushed a deep purple, and returned in a faint voice: вАЬO Nastka! I do entreat you, tell him naught of this letter!вАЭ

вАЬWere it but in print! I can read any book, I know the letters perfectly.вБ†вАФBut I can make nothing of these strokes and crooks and curls.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Just like a fly dipped into ink, and crawling over the paper.вАЭ

вАЬBut, Nastka, youвАЩll not tell him?вАЭ

вАЬOnly yesterday I said to you that I could not be mixed up in this.вБ†вАФBut, if your man is coming home, all will have to come out!вАЭ she added, rising to her feet.

Teresa could not answer a word; the tears she was trying to keep back choked her.

Nastka withdrew, rather out of humour, calling her fowls as she went; and Teresa, making up five ducksвАЩ eggs in a bundle, went to the organistвАЩs.

She was a good while getting there, stopping as she did every while, and slinking about in the shade, and staring at the incomprehensible symbols before her.

вАЬPerchance he is returning.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

She writhed in the grip of dread, her knees shook, her heart throbbed wildly; with misty eyes she staggered on, as one in sore need of succour; more than once she had to lean against the trees not to fall.

вАЬAnd perhaps he is only writing about money!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Her steps began to flag; the letter had become a burden, a torment to her; she was always shifting it from hands to bosom and back again.

Nobody seemed at home at the organistвАЩs. All the doors were open, all the rooms empty. One window had a petticoat hung up in lieu of curtain, and a sound of snoring proceeded thence. She advanced timidly towards the passage, looking round her at the yard. A servant-girl was sitting at the kitchen-door, churning butter and driving flies away with a bough.

вАЬWhereвАЩs your mistress?вАЭ

вАЬIn the garden; yeвАЩll hear her soon!вАЭ

Teresa remained standing there, crushing the letter in her hand, and drawing her kerchief forward over her head, for the sun was now shining directly from above the sheds.

Fowls cackled noisily from the priestвАЩs yard, separated only by a hedge from the other: ducks were riotous in pools, little turkeys plaintively clamorous near the hedge; big gobbling turkey-cocks, with drooping wings, made furious onsets at sucking-pigs wallowing in the mire; and pigeons circled in air, slowly settlingвБ†вАФa snow-white cloudвБ†вАФon the red roof.

TeresaвАЩs eyes were wet. She averted her face to ask:

вАЬIs the organist within?вАЭ

вАЬWhere else? His Reverence is away, so he has lain down to sleep again.вАЭ

вАЬThe priest has gone to the fair, no doubt?вАЭ

вАЬAh, yes; to purchase a bull.вАЭ

вАЬWhat, has he not possessions enough?вАЭ

вАЬWhoso has much wants more,вАЭ the servant grunted.

Teresa was silent awhile. It was hard that she should have so little, and others so much!

вАЬMistress is coming!вАЭ the servant announced, working the dasher up and down in the churn so violently that the cream spurted out.

вАЬвАКвАЩTis all your doing, lazy boy! you let the horse into the clover on purpose because ye had no mind to go as far as the fallows!вАЭ she was heard to scream. вАЬTwo rods of clover eaten! But IвАЩll tell your uncle at once, you good-for-nothing, and youвАЩll get such a beating!вАЭ

вАЬBut I drove it to the fallows myself, I did, and tethered it to the ring!вАЭ

вАЬNo lies! Your uncle will have a talk with you!вАЭ

вАЬBut I tell you, Aunt, I did not drive any horse there.вАЭ

вАЬWho did, then? His Reverence, eh?вАЭ she asked sarcastically.

вАЬYe have guessed, Aunt. Aye, the priest has grazed his horses there,вАЭ the lad replied, raising his voice.

вАЬAre you mad? Hold your tongue, lest someone hear.вАЭ

вАЬI will not! To his face IвАЩll say it!вБ†вАФI went at daybreak to fetch the horses in; the bay one was lying down, the mare feeding: both just where I had left them last night. I loosed them and mounted the bay one, when I saw horses grazing in our clover. It was grey dawn.вБ†вАФI rode on aslant, nigh the priestвАЩs garden, to head them off; so I passed along KlembaвАЩs pathway. And then I saw the priest saying his breviary, and looking round, and whipping his horses further and further into the clover!вАЭ

вАЬHush, Michael!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ What an unheard-of thing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The priest himself!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I always said that last yearвАЩs hay.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But silence; here comes a woman.вАЭ

She waddled in hurriedly, and the organist called for Michael from under his bedclothes.

Teresa handed over her eggs, embraced the goodwifeвАЩs knees, and begged to know the contents of her husbandвАЩs letter.

вАЬJust wait a little.вАЭ

Some time later, they called her into the room. The organist, scantily attiredвБ†вАФin shirt and drawers onlyвБ†вАФwas taking his morning coffee. He began to read to her.

Her heart died within her as she listened. Yes, heвБ†вАФher husbandвБ†вАФwas coming back at harvest-time, along with Kuba Yarchyk of Vola, and Gregory, BorynaвАЩs son! The letter was affectionate: he longed to see her, asked about everyone at home, sent messages to his acquaintances, and felt brimming over with gladness at the thought of returning. Gregory added a few words, asking her to tell his father he was coming. Poor fellow! he little knew what had taken place.

Those kind words smote Teresa like a whip and cut her to the heart. She did her best to bear up against this dreadful news, but her eyes were soon wet and streamed with telltale drops.

вАЬHow pleased she is that her goodman is coming!вАЭ the organistвАЩs wife said, with derisive emphasis.

At the words, she wept yet more abundantly, and took to flight that they should not see her break down yet more. For a long time she went crouching about the hedges.

вАЬWhat shall I do? Oh, what shall I do?вАЭ she cried helplessly, in bitter pain.

Her goodman was comingвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and would learn everything! Terror, like a destroying blast, swept over her at the thought. Her Yasyek was a good-natured fellow, but very impetuous, like all the Ploshkas. Never would he forgive the wrong done; he would kill him. вАЬO Lord, have mercy!вАЭ she cried, but without one thought for herself. Full of tears and rent within her soul, she found her way after a time to BorynaвАЩs cabin. Hanka was out; she had left long since; Yagna was working at her motherвАЩs. Only Yagustynka and Yuzka were at home, spreading out linen to bleach in the orchard.

She told them about Gregory, wishing to get away at once. But the old woman took her aside, and said in a low and unusually kind tone:

вАЬTeresa, control yourself; be reasonable. Evil mouths cannot be prevented from speaking.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Your Yasyek will return, and will know all in any case. Think of it: a lover is but for a month, a husband for life. вАЩTis good advice give.вАЭ

вАЬWhat do ye mean?вАЭ she stammered, feigning not to understand.

вАЬDo not pretend: we all know all about you both. Send Matthew about his business while it is yet time. If ye do, Yasyek will not believe what they say. He has been yearning for you; easily will you make him believe anything! Matthew has had a liking for your bed, but is not bound to it: get rid of him while you can.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A love! it passes by in a moment, as yesterday has passed: were you to lay down your life for it, it would not stay. A loveвБ†вАФвАЩtis but a Sunday dainty: who feeds on it daily will care not for it at all.вБ†вАФFolk say: вАШLove of heart makes us smart: when we wed, then weвАЩre dead!вАЩвБ†вАФIt may be; but death with a goodman and children is better than wild outlawed freedom.вБ†вАФDo not whimper, but save yourself while it still is time. What if your man should cease to love you, because of this your deed, and chase you from his home? Whither should you go? To your ruin: to be the laughingstock of everyone! Fool! Every man has breeches: Matthew and Kuba, both alike: everyone swears the same oaths, and is sweet as honey so long as his fondness endures.вБ†вАФNow think well, and take to heart what I say; for I, your aunt, wish you well.вАЭ

But Teresa would hear no more. She fled away into the fields, and sat down in the rye to give a free course to her grief.

But it was in vain that she attempted to think over YagustynkaвАЩs advice. Her passion for Matthew was such that the very idea of giving him up made her writhe on the ground like a wounded beast.

Some time elapsed before the sounds of a quarrel close at hand made her start to her feet.

Just in front of the VoytвАЩs cottage a fierce brawl was in progress.

The VoytвАЩs wife and Kozlova were loading each other with the fiercest invectives.

Opposite to one another they stood, with the road and their respective enclosures between them, clad only in their smocks and petticoats, each panting with fury, reviling the other with all her might, and shaking her fists at her.

The Voyt was loading his cart, and now and then glancing at a peasant from Modlitsa, who, sitting in the porch, enjoyed the scene with keen relish, and set the women on.

The cries were heard afar; and soon many a head peeped from behind the neighbouring hedges and hut-corners.

And, Lord! how they both stormed! The VoytвАЩs wife, usually so quiet and mild-tempered, was terribly on the rampage today, and her rage waxed higher every minute; and Kozlova, as of set purpose, taunted and mocked her, omitting nothing to increase her fury.

вАЬTalk, talk, talk, my lady Voyt!вАЭ she called out; вАЬtalk away! ThereвАЩs no dog can outbark your ladyship!вАЭ

вАЬNot a week goes by, but something is missing from my premises! Laying hensвБ†вАФchickensвБ†вАФeven an old gooseвБ†вАФhave disappeared. Aye, in my garden, in my orchard, I have untold losses to put up with! Ah, may the wrong done to me poison you! May it choke you to death!вАЭ

вАЬVery good!вБ†вАФScreech, old crow! Screech, my lady Voyt! вАЩTwill soothe you!вАЭ

вАЬWhy, this very day,вАЭ she said, addressing Teresa, who stood in the road, вАЬI had taken five pieces of linen out into the orchard to bleach.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ After breakfast, behold, I come out to sprinkle themвБ†вАФone is missing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I seek it!вБ†вАФSwallowed by the earth, as it were!вБ†вАФAnd lo, I had weighted it with stones; and of wind there was none at all!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Good linen, fine linen! Ye could get none better at any shopвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and behold, it is gone!вАЭ

вАЬYour eyelids are so puffed out with fat, you could not see it!вАЭ

вАЬI could not, for you, thief, have stolen it!вАЭ she vociferated.

вАЬI, a thief!вБ†вАФSay, oh, say that once more!вАЭ

вАЬYou thief! you thief! Thereto, and before all men, will I bear witness! And that youвАЩll confess when I take you to jail in irons!вАЭ

вАЬSheвБ†вАФshe has called me a thief!вБ†вАФPeople, have ye heard? As thereвАЩs a God, IвАЩll bring an action for that.вБ†вАФYe all have heard her. What, have I robbed you, you blockhead? where are your witnesses?вАЭ

At that, the VoytвАЩs wife snatched up a stake, and dashed out into the road with the rush of an infuriated dog, shrieking: вАЬIвАЩll witness it on you with my stick! IвАЩll prove it! IвАЩll.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬCome on, my lady Voyt! Yah! only touch me, you pig! Only touch me, you scarecrow of a bitch!вАЭ she cried, running forward likewise.

She pushed aside her goodman, who would have kept her back, and with legs wide apart and arms akimbo, called out jeering:

вАЬStrike me, strike me, my lady Voyt, and youвАЩll lie in jail for it!вАЭ

вАЬHold your peace, woman!вАЭ the Voyt interposed, вАЬor IвАЩll send you off to jail first!вАЭ

вАЬLock up your own mad dog, вАЩtis your duty; tie up your wife with a rope, lest she bite!вАЭ Kozlova screamed, exasperated.

вАЬWoman!вАЭ he shouted, threatening her. вАЬWhen I speak, do respect my office!вАЭ

вАЬI spit on your officeвАЭвБ†вАФbut the words used were bolder farвБ†вАФвАЬdo you understand me? He threaten, he?вБ†вАФLook at him! He may just as well have stolen the stuff himself to get his light-oвАЩ-love a smock! Why, the money of the community is all gone that way; you have drunk it all, you toss-pot!вБ†вАФOh, we know about your doings, never fear!вБ†вАФAye, you too, my lord the Voyt, will lie in jail!вАЭ

This was the last straw: both of them flew at her like wolves. The VoytвАЩs wife hit her first with her stick across the face, and then, with a savage cry, went at her with her nails, while the Voyt belaboured her wherever he could find a place.

Bartek flew instantly to the rescue of his wife.

They all three closed together like fighting dogs; no one could tell whose fists, whose heads, were seen whirling through the air, nor whose voices were bellowing. From fence to road, from road to fence, they went staggering and tossing like sheaves blown along in a great wind, till in their fury of fighting they fell to the ground on a sandheap.

Plunging in a cloud of dust, they were audible still with their imprecations and invectives; but presently they got out into the road, fighting and shrieking at the top of their voices.

At times one or other of them fell apart, and now and then they were all upon their feet; then, clutching one another by the hair, or the throat, or the nape of the neck, they began the battle again.

But all the village was soon aroused by the noise; women came hovering helplessly around the battlefield; and at last men arrived and separated the combatants.

But the curses and imprecations and wailings and threats went on, and were beyond all description. The neighbours made off at once, fearing they might be called in to bear witness: but throughout the village the whisper went that the Voyt and his wife had given the Koziols a fearful thrashing.

A few minutes later, the Voyt, with a swollen face, drove off accompanied by his wife, also much battered and scratched, to depose against their enemies.

About an hour afterwards, the Koziols started likewise: old Ploshka having very kindly offered to take them to town gratisвБ†вАФto pay out the Voyt for siding with the Squire.

They went to make their complaint, just as they were at the end of the fight, and without doing anything to make themselves more presentable.

They drove slowly through the village, telling everybody, as they went, about their ill-treatment, and showing the wounds received.

KoziolвАЩs head was cut open to the bone, so that his face, neck, and breast, visible through a rent in his shirt, were all covered with blood. The hurt was in reality not great; but at every moment he would press his side and moan:

вАЬMy God! I can bear no more! He has broken every one of my ribs!вБ†вАФHelp, good people, help! or I die!вАЭ

His wife then took up the lament.

вАЬHe took a club to beat him with!вБ†вАФAh, poor man! be easy; you have had much to suffer, but thereвАЩs justice to punish ruffians, there is!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yes, he meant to slay my man, and folk had hard work to prevent him from doing it: as they will all testify in court.вАЭ These explanations she would frequently interrupt with dreadful howls. Indeed, she was disfigured almost past recognition: bareheaded, with tufts of hair torn out scalp and all, her ears torn and bleeding, her eyes running blood, and her whole face clawed, scratched, harrowed like a field. And though all knew well what a вАЬdaisyвАЭ the woman was, the sight roused pity in many a heart.

вАЬDear, dear! вАЩtwas too bad to treat them so horribly!вАЭ

вАЬA sin and a shame it is! They have been well-nigh killed.вАЭ

вАЬAye, they have been frightfully beaten. But is aught forbidden to my lord the Voyt?вБ†вАФSuch an official, such a great man?вАЭ Ploshka put in maliciously, addressing the people.

They were completely bewildered, and remained astounded and upset long after the Koziols had passed out of sight.

Teresa, who had taken cover during the fray, did not show her face till both parties had gone.

She looked in at the KoziolsвАЩ, Bartek being distantly related to her. No one was in; but the three little children that Kozlova had just brought with her from Warsaw, sat outside the cabin, huddled together, greedily devouring some half-boiled potatoes, defending their food from the pigs with their spoons, and crying out at them. They were so wretched, so miserably neglected, so filthily dirty, that her heart pitied them, and she took them into the passage, and shut them in from their foes; then she ran off to tell the news.

At the GolabsвАЩ, there was no one but Nastka.

Matthew had, before breakfast, gone over to Staho (BylitsaвАЩs son-in-law) to look over the ruined hut and see if anything could be done with it. The old man, too, was with him, now and then stammering a word or two. Mr.¬†Yacek, sitting as usual at the threshold, smoked a cigarette and whistled to the doves that circled about over the cherry-trees.

Noontide was not far off.

The heated air was quivering over the fields like rippling water; the fields and orchard-grounds basked delightedly in the sunbeams; now and again, a blossom fell from BylitsaвАЩs cherry-trees, flickering down like a white little butterfly.

It was well past noon when Matthew had finished his examination. While still poking the timbers here and there, he gave sentence:

вАЬвАКвАЩTis all rotten wood, crumbling to dust: ye can build nothing with it. Quite useless.вАЭ

вАЬPerchance,вАЭ Staho said anxiously, вАЬI might buy some new timber, and then.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYeвАЩd require timber for a whole cabin. Not one beam here is fit for aught.вАЭ

вАЬGracious heavens!вАЭ

вАЬBut the lower beams may hold yet,вАЭ old Bylitsa faltered; вАЬwe should only have to get new upper timbers, and clamp the woodwork together and prop it up.вАЭ

вАЬThen do so, if ye be so clever! I do not build with touchwood!вАЭ he retorted, putting on his coat.

Here Veronka, lamenting bitterly, and holding a child in her arms, came on the scene.

вАЬWhat, alas! what shall we do now?вАЭ

вАЬA new hut,вАЭ said Staho in sore distress, вАЬwould cost about two thousand roubles.вБ†вАФYet we might get some timber from our forest; and I could manage for the rest.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ An application to the Government Board.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬBut the forest is now in the hands of the law court: what timber would they give us just now? Why, we are even forbidden to gather wood there for fuel! Wait till a sentence is given, and then build!вАЭ was MatthewвАЩs advice.

вАЬIndeed! Very fine!вБ†вАФAnd where shall we dwell this winter, I ask?вАЭ said Veronka, with a fresh burst of tears.

No more was said. Matthew put his tools together, while Staho scratched his head, and Bylitsa blew his nose round the corner.

In that moment, Mr.¬†Yacek stood up, and raising his voice:

вАЬWeep not, Veronka,вАЭ he said; вАЬthe timber for your cabin shall be found!вАЭ

All stood open-mouthed, lost in amazement; till Matthew, recovering himself first, burst out with a loud guffaw.

вАЬClever men promise, fools believe them!вБ†вАФHe has not where to lay his head, yet talks of giving cabins to others!вАЭ he cried roughly, staring at him under his bent brows; but Mr.¬†Yacek sat down again, went on smoking and playing with his beard, his eyes fixed on the skyline.

вАЬIn a little, he will promise you a whole farm!вАЭ Matthew said, with a laugh and a shrug, as he left them.

He at once turned to the left along the path that led to the outhouses.

Few were at work in the gardens that day: there was but the red glint of a stray petticoat, or the sight of a man here and there mending a roof, or pottering about the granary gates, open to the fields.

Matthew was in no hurry; he loitered about willingly, chatting with the neighbours on the topic of the VoytвАЩs battle, grinning and talking merrily with lasses, or making such strong-flavoured jokes with the elder women in the gardens that they could not help laughing; many a one sighed and cast fond looks after him, as he passed out of sight.

And a handsome fellow indeed he was: built like an oak, and the king, as it were, of all the young men in Lipka; first in strength (after Antek Boryna); and as a dancer, not inferior to Staho Ploshka. And withal, a man of much ability in every field of work; able to construct a cart, to set up a chimney, to repair a cabin; and he played the flute beautifully too. So, though he had next to no land at all, and was so openhanded with everybody that he never put anything by, many a mother would have gladly drunk with him the price of a calf, could she but have disposed him favourably thereby towards marriage with her daughter; more than one girl, too, had allowed him much intimacy, hoping to get the banns sooner published.

All would not do. He drank with the mothers, made love to the daughters, but on the question of marriage was slippery as an eel.

вАЬвАКвАЩTis hard to choose.вБ†вАФEach one has her good points; and others are growing up, worth more than any.вБ†вАФIвАЩll wait,вАЭ he would say, when matchmakers beset him.

Then, the preceding winter, he had that entanglement with Teresa, and lived almost openly with her, disregarding both gossip and cautions.

вАЬWhen Yasyek comes, IвАЩll give her back to him, and heвАЩll stand me treat into the bargain for having taken good care of her,вАЭ he once said laughingly, shortly after his return. He was tired of her, and slowly drifting apart.

And now, as he went in to dinner, he chose a longer way round, merely to joke with the girls, and have some horseplay with those who would let him.

And thus, quite unexpectedly, he came face to face with Yagna, weeding her motherвАЩs garden.

вАЬAh! Yagna!вАЭ he cried out, joyfully.

She suddenly shot up to her full height, tall and graceful as a hollyhock.

вАЬSo youвАЩve noticed me? Oh, how very soon! Not more than a week since your homecoming!вАЭ

вАЬWhy, you look lovelier than ever before!вАЭ he said in low tones of wonder.

Her dress was tucked up to the knees; and beneath her red kerchief, knotted under her chin, those great sweet turquoise eyes of hers looked out, her white teeth gleamed between cherry lips, all her face glowed apple-redвБ†вАФand so fair, it seemed asking to be kissed.

She boldly set her arms akimbo, and shot such irresistibly bright glances at him as thrilled him through and through. Looking round carefully, he drew nearer.

вАЬFor a whole week I have been seeking youвБ†вАФin vain!вАЭ

вАЬTell a dog lies: it may believe you.вБ†вАФHa! the man goes grinning about the gardens every evening; every evening he flatters another girl: and now heвАЩll dare tell me вАЩtis not true?вАЭ

вАЬWhy, Yagna, is this your greeting to me?вАЭ

вАЬBetter fall on my knees and thank you for remembering I exist, eh?вАЭ

вАЬLast year I had another welcome!вАЭ

вАЬBut this is not last year!вАЭ She turned away from him and hid her face. He at once stepped forward, clasping her with eager arms.

She tore herself in anger from his grasp.

вАЬLet me alone; Teresa would tear my eyes out because of you!вАЭ

вАЬYagna!вАЭ he sighed.

вАЬGet back; make love to her, the soldierвАЩs wife; render her every service till he come back to her.вБ†вАФYou were in prison, and she fed you well: now must you make her some return!вАЭ

Each word of hers was like a blow, and uttered with such scorn that Matthew, taken aback, could find no answer.

Shame took hold of him; he turned a dusky red, hung his head and took to flight at once.

Though Yagna had but told him what she felt and had been feeling all the week, she now regretted her words. Never had she expected he would have been so offended as to leave her.

вАЬFoolish one! I spoke but out of spite!вАЭ she thought, gazing sorrowfully after him. вАЬTo be so angry all at once with me!вБ†вАФMatthew!вАЭ

Rushing away through the orchard as if running for his life, he did not hear her call.

вАЬThat wasp! that vixen!вАЭ he growled, making straight for home now, anger and astonishment alternately uppermost in his mind. Before, she had always been so sweet, so mild! And now she had treated him like dirt. He felt the shame so keenly that he looked round to make sure no one had heard.

вАЬAnd she reminded me of Teresa! Silly thing!вБ†вАФTeresa is naught to meвБ†вАФnaught but a toy!вБ†вАФHow her eyes blazed! In what a posture she set her arms!вБ†вАФAh, it were no shame to be stung by such a oneвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ if only the honey were forthcoming afterwards.вАЭ He was now close to his hut, and slackened his pace.

вАЬShe was angry that I alluded to the past.вБ†вАФBut was I in the wrong?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ As to TeresaвАЭвБ†вАФhere he made a wry face, as one who has gulped down vinegarвБ†вАФвАЬI have enough of that crybaby. I have taken no oath to remain with her, have I?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The tail sticks to the cow, but I am no cowвАЩs tail!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And then she has a goodman of her own; and I might get a public rebuke from the pulpit on her account.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Such a woman is the ruin of a man.вБ†вАФTo the devil with women!вАЭ he concluded, in a most cantankerous mood.

Dinner was not ready at home. He scolded his sister for dawdling, and went in to Teresa, who was milking in the orchard, and raised to him very sad tearful eyes.

вАЬWhimpering again? And wherefore?вАЭ

She excused herself, looking on him fondly.

вАЬPay more attention: the udder squirts milk upon your petticoat.вАЭ

Why was he so unkind, so hard to her today? she wondered. What had come over him? She was as gentle as could possibly be; yet he snapped her up fiercely at every word she said.

He seemed looking about the orchard for something, but ever and anon flung a furtive glance at her, and wondered more and more.

вАЬWhere had I mine eyes?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Such a paltry flabby thing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Neither beauty nor savour!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Rawboned and sour-tasting!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Black as a gipsy, too; and of carriage, none whatever!вАЭ

True, her eyes, and they alone, were beautiful; perhaps as much so as YagnaвАЩs: large, bright as a blue sky, and peering under black brows. But as often as he met them, he turned aside and swore softly to himself.

вАЬShe rolls her eyes like a calf!вАЭ

Those looks of hers made him impatient and angry.

вАЬI will not see them, I will not!вБ†вАФYes, yes, ogle as much as you like, ye will not catch me.вАЭ

They had dinner together, but he never spoke once to her, nor so much as glanced in her direction. To Nastka he spoke, indeed, but no pleasant words.

вАЬA dog would not touch these groats: they are disgustingly burnt!вАЭ

вАЬOnly a little: just enough to have a taste.вАЭ

вАЬDo not cross me!вБ†вАФAnd ye have put more flies than bacon-scratchings in them!вАЭ

вАЬWhat, do you object to flies now? DonвАЩt be so very dainty! They will not poison you.вАЭ

The cabbage dish, he then complained, had been cooked with rancid lard.

вАЬYe might as well have seasoned it with axle-grease!вАЭ

вАЬYou donвАЩt know how that would taste? I do not, nor will I try,вАЭ she returned sharply.

But he continued to seek every occasion to grumble. Teresa was dumb all the time; so he set at her directly after dinner, seeing her cow rubbing herself at the corner of the hut.

вАЬSheвАЩs filthy with dung, crusted all over: can ye not rub her down?вАЭ

вАЬOur byre is wet, and she gets dirty there.вАЭ

вАЬWet, indeed!вАЭ he vociferated. вАЬThere are pine-boughs enough in the forest for dry fodder: but ye must wait for someone to collect them and bring them here to you. And the beasts might rot their flanks off with the dung meanwhile.вБ†вАФSo many women in the hut, yet not a stiverвАЩs worth of cleanliness!вАЭ

But Teresa never answered back; she durst not defend herself, and only begged his mercy with her eyes.

She was quiet and obedient, and as hardworking as an ant: she felt even glad to see him so masterful and high-handed with her! Which was precisely what angered him yet more. Those loving timorous eyes enraged him; so did her quiet footsteps, her humble mien, her way of following him about. He was on the point of crying out: вАЬAway from my sight!вАЭ

вАЬBlood of a dog!вБ†вАФPlague take it all!вАЭ he exclaimed at last; and, taking up his tools, without any after-dinner rest, he passed over to the KlembasвАЩ, where he had something to do about the hut.

They were all out in the yard, and at dinner still.

He sat down by the wall for a smoke.

The Klembas were talking of Gregory BorynaвАЩs return from the army.

вАЬWhat? home so soon?вАЭ he asked.

вАЬWhy, know ye not?вАЭ said old Klemba. вАЬTogether with Yasyek, TeresaвАЩs goodman, and Yarchyk from Vola.вАЭ

вАЬThey are to be here in harvest-time. This morning, Teresa went to get the letter read by the organist, who told me about it.вБ†вАФThatвАЩs news for you, that Yasyek is returning!вАЭ he blurted out.

A silence followed. All eyes were fixed on vacancy, and the women, stifling their desire to laugh, turned very red. He gave no heed, and seemed pleased at the news, remarking tranquilly:

вАЬвАКвАЩTis well that he returns: now they will peradventure cease from slandering Teresa.вАЭ

Their spoons stopped, suspended in air over the dish, so confounded were they all. He looked round him, unabashed, and then added:

вАЬYe are well aware how little they spare her. She is naught to me but a distant relative on my fatherвАЩs side. But if any dirty sneak should hint at anything else, IвАЩd stop his mouth so that heвАЩd never forget it! But women are the worst of all; they never spare another woman. Be she white as snow, they would find means to foul her!вАЭ

вАЬQuite so, quite so,вАЭ they replied, with their eyes upon the dish.

вАЬHave ye been at BorynaвАЩs yet?вАЭ he inquired, anxiously.

вАЬI have long been about to go; but something always prevented me.вАЭ

вАЬHe suffers for us all, and weвБ†вАФwe forget him!вАЭ

вАЬAnd youвБ†вАФhave you looked in there?вАЭ

вАЬI?вБ†вАФShould I go in by myself, folk would say I was after Yagna!вАЭ

вАЬAs particular as a girl after she has tripped!вАЭ muttered old Agata, who sat by the hedge, with a small bowl on her knees.

вАЬWell, but I have enough of all this yelping.вАЭ

Klemba laughed. вАЬThe wolf,вАЭ he said, вАЬchanges his life when his teeth are gone.вАЭ

вАЬOr,вАЭ Matthew added, вАЬwhen he thinks of settling down.вАЭ

вАЬHo, ho! we shall soon have you sending messengers to some lass?вАЭ young Klemba cried in high glee.

вАЬAye, I am weighing the matter seriously.вАЭ

вАЬMake your choice quickly, Matthew, and ask me to be your bridesmaid!вАЭ piped Kate, the eldest daughter.

вАЬAh, but thereвАЩs the rub. All are equally excellent, and everyoneвАЩs better than the other. MagdaвАЩs the richest, but sheвАЩs toothless and blear-eyed; UlisiaвАЩs a flower, but has one hip too big, and no dowry but a barrel of sauerkraut; Franka has a baby; Mary is too friendly to all the boys; Eva has a hundred zloty, all in coppers; but sheвАЩs a sluggard, always lying abed. All would fain eat fat things, drink sweet things, and do nothing. Oh, they are pure gold, these girls! And others there are besides, nice, but not yet grown up.вАЭ

They all laughed till the pigeons flew off the roof.

вАЬI say true. Till she is grown up, I care naught for a girl, be she ever so comely.вАЭ

Here Dame Klemba rebuked him for talking of such things.

вАЬOh, I am only joking. And girls like these jokes, they say, as well as any.вАЭ

At this the lasses were offended, and grew red as turkeys in angry protest.

вАЬA fine fellow he is: not one of us is good enough for him!вАЭ

вАЬIf there are none in Lipka you can like, then take one from elsewhere!вАЭ they cried.

вАЬBut there are, there are! вАЩTis easier to find an old maid here than a silver zloty. Ah, how many there are! They every Saturday beautify themselves at daybreak very thoroughly, and braid their hair, and chase chickens through the orchard to barter with the Jew for vodka, and are all the afternoon on the watch for messengers bringing a proposal. Why, have I not seen them on the roofs, waving their kerchiefs at me, and shouting: вАШCome to me, Matthew, come!вАЩ?вБ†вАФAnd the mothers, too, taking up the cry: вАШTo Kate first, Matthew, to Kate! IвАЩll add to her portionвБ†вАФa cheese and eight eggs: Matthew, come to Kate!вАЩвАКвАЭ

The men were near splitting their sides with laughter, he amused them so; but the girls, indignant, made such an uproar that old Klemba interposed:

вАЬHush, girls! yeвАЩre as noisy as magpies before rain.вАЭ

The din went on nevertheless. So, to end the squabble, he inquired:

вАЬWere you present, Matthew, at the VoytвАЩs affray?вАЭ

вАЬNot I. But the Koziols were very soundly swinged, they say.вАЭ

вАЬAye, swinged with a vengeance! They were awful to behold.вБ†вАФWell, well, the Voyt has had his fling, iвАЩ faith.вАЭ

вАЬHe has waxed fat on the CommuneвАЩs bread, and now he plays pranks!вАЭ

вАЬAye, indeed, he fears none. Who would stand up to him? Any other should pay dear for such sportвБ†вАФheвАЩll not lose a hair thereby. He knows men in office, and can do as he pleases here.вАЭ

вАЬBecause ye are but sheep to let him. He puts you down, and sets himself above you all!вАЭ

вАЬHaving chosen him ourselves, we must respect his rule.вАЭ

вАЬBut they that set him up can put him down again.вАЭ

вАЬHush, Matthew, not so loud: folk may hear your words.вАЭ

вАЬвБ†вАФAnd tell him. Then heвАЩll know what I have said.вБ†вАФLet him fall foul of me, though, if he dare!вАЭ

вАЬMatthias, who alone could have held against him, is at deathвАЩs door. No one else would put himself forward: each has troubles enough of his own,вАЭ the old man concluded, rising from his seat.

All rose with him, and went, some to rest awhile, some to stretch their legs and loosen their girdles; othersвБ†вАФthe lassesвБ†вАФto wash the dishes in the pond, and take a little recreation. But Matthew went directly to set up props and stays for the cabin, while Klemba lit his pipe and sat on the doorstep.

As he puffed, his mind reverted to the late talk. вАЬWho stands up for others, shall have many bothers!вАЭ he growled.

The sun was high above the cabin, the afternoon hot. The orchards stood motionless, the sunlight trembled through their rustling leaves, and many a petal floated down upon the grass. Bees buzzed among the apple-tree boughs; athwart the greenery, the millpond shimmered, and the birds were all hushed. A pleasant afternoon drowsiness pervaded the place.

Klemba, who wanted to keep awake, sauntered over to his potato-pit.

A little later he returned, puffing hard at his extinguished pipe, and spluttering, and throwing back the long hair that had fallen over his brow.

вАЬHave you seen?вАЭ his wife asked him, peeping out at the door.

вАЬI have. Our potatoes will only last us till harvest, if we cook them but once a day!вАЭ

вАЬOnce a day only!вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs to be done? We are so many!вБ†вАФTen hungry mouths, the ravenous maws beneath them!вБ†вАФWe must think of something.вАЭ

вАЬNot of the heifer, at any rate. I tell you, I will not have her sold. Do what ye will, the cattle must not go!вАЭ

He waved his hands, as if to drive away an importunate wasp; when she had gone, he lit his pipe again.

вАЬA pigheaded old thing!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If needs must.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A heiferвАЩs no holy thing to die for!вАЭ

The sun now shone in his eyes; he only turned his back, and puffed more slowly. Loosening his girdle (the meal of potatoes lay heavy upon him), he began to nod. The doves cooed on the thatch, and the leaves were quivering with slumberous murmurs.

вАЬThomas!вАЭ

It was AgataвАЩs voice. He opened his eyes. She was sitting beside him, with an anxious look.

вАЬThese months before harvest are hard times for you,вАЭ she said. вАЬIf ye are willing, I have a little money: ye could take it. I was keeping it for my burial; but if ye are so hard pressed, IвАЩll lend it. Why sell the heifer? I stood by when she was dropped; sheвАЩs of a good race of milkers. Perchance I may, God willing, live on till harvestвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and then ye will give the money back. вАЩTis no shame, even for a farmer, to take from his own folk, when in need. So here!вАЭ And she pressed into his hand three roubles, all in silver zloty.

вАЬNay, take it back: I shall manage.вАЭ

вАЬHere, I can add half a rouble more. Take it,вАЭ she begged him in a whisper.

вАЬNay. But thanks notwithstanding. It is exceeding kind of you.вАЭ

вАЬThere, then: here are thirty zloty all told: pray take them!вАЭ She looked into her moneybag, counting out five-kopek bits and keeping down her tears. It was a hard sacrifice for her; every coin she told out gave her a sharp pang.

The money glittered very temptingly in the sun. His eyes shone with desire as he gloated over them; they were all new bright pieces. But, heaving a deep sigh, he mastered himself with a great effort, and said to her:

вАЬHide all that carefully, or folk may see it, and peradventure rob you.вАЭ

She still went on imploring him in a low voice; but he said no more, and she slowly put her treasures back again.

вАЬWherefore will ye not dwell with us?вАЭ he asked her, after a while.

вАЬHow can I? I am good for nothing, not even to follow the geeseвБ†вАФI am very weak, expecting my end day by day. It would truly be more pleasant to die amongst oneвАЩs own kinsfolk: much more. Yea, even in the stall where the heifer has been.вБ†вАФAnd I have forty zloty ready for my burial: perhaps вАЩtwould also suffice for a MassвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ as befits a farmerвАЩs kinswoman!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I should leave my featherbed to you.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Fear ye not: I should fall asleep quietly amongst you, and sooner than ye expect; very soon.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ she faltered anxiously, waiting with a throbbing heart for him to say: вАЬStay with us!вАЭ

He did not, but made as if he had not followed the drift of her rambling talk; and stretched himself, and yawned, and walked uneasily about before the hut, the barn, the hayrick.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

She sobbed and moaned plaintively. вАЬHow could he, indeed? He, a husbandman of such reputeвБ†вАФand I, a wretched beggar-woman!вАЭ

And thereupon she started on her daily search for some place in the village where she could die in good odour, as a respectable peasant-woman.

On and on she crawled to find some such nook, and ever drifting about like gossamer in the gale, to stick fast no one knows where.

People joked about her, and enjoyed saying that she ought to stay with her family, and telling the Klembas, with mock friendship:

вАЬWhy, she is of your family, and she has money for her funeral besides, and then she will not trouble you very long. Where else should she be but with you?вАЭ

Dame Klemba thought of those words, when her goodman told her at night of what Agata had said that day. They were in bed at the time, their children had begun to snore, and she whispered to persuade him:

вАЬThere will be room for her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ She may lie in the hay.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Or we shall drive the geese out into the shed.вБ†вАФFor her food, she wants next to naught.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And she cannot drag on very long.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Moreover, she will be buried at her own cost.вБ†вАФSo people will not speak against us.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And then, the featherbed would be ours: we could not easily get such another one.вАЭ This she pointed out to him very eagerly.

In answer, Klemba merely snored at the time; but in the morning he said:

вАЬIf Agata were quite destitute, I should take her in; it were then GodвАЩs will, and I could not do otherwise. But, as things stand, they will say we have taken her in for the sake of what she leaves us. As it is, they blame us for having let her go and beg.вБ†вАФNay, it cannot be.вАЭ

Klembova obeyed her husband in all things, but she sighed bitterly over the featherbed lost to them, and got up to hurry the girls out to their work; for that day they had to plant the cabbages.

It was then May weather at its best. The breeze blew, stirring the corn in rippling waves. The orchards whispered as they waved, and shook down their blossoming petals; and heavy clusters of lilac and bird-cherry blossoms filled the air with perfume. From the fields, songs came borne on the wind; and in the smithy, the hammer rang on the anvil. Since morning, the roads had been full of people and racket, and the women wended their way to the cabbage patches, bearing young plants with them in sieves and baskets.

Before the morning dew was quite dry, the black fields, cut into many a furrow full of water sparkling in the sunshine, were dappled all over with red aprons and skirts.

Dame Klemba went with her daughters, the while her goodman with his sons were helping Matthew to repair their cabin.

Old Klemba, however, presently finding the sun too hot, called Balcerek and went over to see Boryna.

вАЬA fine day, friend,вАЭ he said, taking a pinch from BalcerekвАЩs snuffbox.

вАЬSplendid. But letвАЩs hope this great heat will not last.вАЭ

вАЬRain is falling on all sides: our turn must come soon.вАЭ

вАЬYet it looks like drought: the trees are covered with insects.вАЭ

вАЬAnd the vegetables, how late they have come up! A drought would destroy them. But, by GodвАЩs grace, it may not come to that.вАЭ

вАЬWell, and what of the fair? Any news of your horse?вАЭ

вАЬI gave the police officer three roubles, and he gave me promises.вАЭ

вАЬWe have naught in safety! We live in constant alarm, as hares do, and thereвАЩs no help for it.вАЭ

вАЬOur Voyt is a mere figurehead,вАЭ suggested Balcerek, in low cautious tones.

вАЬWe ought,вАЭ Klemba rapped out, вАЬto seek another.вАЭ

Balcerek gave him a warning look; but he went on, excitedly:

вАЬHe brings the village to shame.вБ†вАФHave ye heard what he did yesterday?вАЭ

вАЬOh, his brawl? thatвАЩs naught.вБ†вАФBut thereвАЩs more, and we may have to pay dear for his spell in office.вАЭ

вАЬBut there are checks on him: the cashier, the scrivener, the rest of the council.вАЭ

вАЬLike dogs set to watch over meat! Aye, theyвАЩll watch so well that we peasants will have in the end to pay for their negligence.вАЭ

вАЬWhatвАЩs to be done?вБ†вАФAny other news?вАЭ

Balcerek spat and tossed up his hand; he cared little to talk, being a cross-grained man, and henpecked besides, which made him still more taciturn.

They arrived at BorynaвАЩs. In the porch, Yuzka was peeling potatoes.

вАЬYe may go in; Father is lying there alone. Hanka is out planting cabbages, and Yagna working at her motherвАЩs.вАЭ

The room looked very empty. A lilac-bough now and then peeped in at the window, and the sunlight filtered in through the verdure outside.

The old man lay as usual, but much emaciated, with his grey beard bristling all over his discoloured cheeks. His head was still bandaged, and his ashen lips were moving as if to speak.

They greeted him. No answer, no movement in reply.

вАЬDo ye not know us?вАЭ said Klemba, taking his hand.

He seemed totally unconscious, or lost in rapt listening to the twittering swallows, building their nests under his thatch, or the brustling of the leaves against the wall outside.

вАЬMatthias!вАЭ Klemba said, shaking him slightly.

The sick man started, his eyelids quivered, he looked round at them.

вАЬDo ye hear?вБ†вАФThis is Klemba, this Balcerek, your companions: surely ye know us!вАЭ

They waited a little, gazing into his eyes.

вАЬBoys!вАЭ he cried suddenly, in a thundering voice. вАЬSee me here alone! To the rescue! Smite them, those sons of dogs! smite them!вАЭ He raised his arms as to ward off a blow, and fell back on his bed.

At the cry, Yuzka rushed in, and put fresh moist bandages on his head. And now, once more, he lay quite motionless: in his wide-open eyes there gleamed an expression of intense fear.

They went away, distressed and disheartened.

вАЬWhy,вАЭ said Klemba, вАЬhe is no longer a living manвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ heвАЩs a corpse!вАЭ

VitekвАЩs stork was stalking about the orchard; the wind now and then swept the branches into the open windows to shade them.

Back they went, plunged in sad silent thought, as if they had been to visit a grave.

вАЬWe must all come to this!вАЭ Klemba said at last.

вАЬTruly,вАЭ sighed the other. вАЬHe has perished, and others gain thereby.вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАШA goat dies once, and thenвБ†вАФnever again!вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬWe too shall follow him shortly.вАЭ

And they looked with hardening eyes at the world around; at the waving corn; at the forest, clear-cut and plain in the distance; at the green-growing fields, and at the bright warm day of springtide: and their spirits settled down in a stony resignation to the will of God.

вАЬNo; man cannot shun what is to be.вАЭ

And so they parted.

Others, too, both on that day and afterwards, came to visit the dying man: but he recognized no one, and at last they came no longer.

The priest had said: вАЬAll he needs is prayers for a speedy departure.вАЭ

And as everyone was full of his own cares and troubles, they naturally forgot him, or spoke of him as of one dead.

Who was there, indeed, to take thought of him?

There were even days when he remained without a drop of water, and might have died of starvation, but for the kind heart of Vitek, who would snatch up anything he could get and take it to вАЬMasterвАЭ; and he would sometimes milk the cows in secret, and bring him to drink. He was indeed filled with attention and respect for the sufferer, and with disquietude also: which made him at last question Pete:

вАЬIsвАЩt true that whosoever dies without confession must go to hell?вАЭ

вАЬMost true. Why, the priest tells us so in church ever so often.вАЭ

вАЬThenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Master shall also go to hell?вАЭ And he crossed himself in dismay.

вАЬHeвАЩs a man like any other!вАЭ

вАЬWhat? Master a man like any other?вАЭ

вАЬYou are as senseless as a cabbage-head!вАЭ said Pete, growing angry; for he saw that Vitek did not believe him.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

And thus did the days go by at BorynaвАЩs cabin.

The village meanwhile, on account of the VoytвАЩs battle, was in a state of great animation, each party eagerly looking for witnesses in its favour.

It was of no great importance in itself: yet the Voyt exerted himself with might and main; and his influence in Lipka proved so considerable that more than half the inhabitants were on his side. They knew him to be no saint; but he was their Voyt all the same, and able to make things hot for opponents. So, by dint of insistence, flattery, and vodka, he got together as many witnesses as he required.

Koziol was very ill in bed, and the priest had been to him with the last sacraments. As to that illness, opinions differed: some went so far as to whisper low that it was only a feint to give the Voyt more trouble.вБ†вАФBut who knew really what to think of it?

In the meantime Kozlova was going about all day, telling the people that she had sold her sow and farrow to purchase medicines for her goodman; and well-nigh daily she stationed herself outside the VoytвАЩs house, loading him with invectives, shrieking that her Bartek was about to die, and calling God and all honest people to witness in her favour and take sides with her.

Only the riffraff of the community, however, and a few tenderhearted women, were with her; Kobus also, a third-rate farmer and a most litigious and quarrelsome man. The others would not listen to her. Some paid no notice to what she said; others, in her own interest, advised her to make friends with the Voyt.

Many broils arose thence; for Kobus had an ungovernable tongue, and was very ready with his fists; while the women, on their side, used extremely violent language. Their anger and acrimony were most intense: for how could they hope to get the better of the farmers and the Voyt together?

In the end, the Jew himself treated the Koziols with contempt, and refused them credit.

Within a week after the battle, everyone had enough of the affair, and the complaints and lamentations connected therewith, and would not listen any longer.

But at that juncture, new helpers came, and the village was again in an uproar.

Ploshka, joining forces with the miller, now stood up openly and strongly for the Koziols.

Not that they cared for their cause in the least: each had his own aims and sought his own advantage.

Ploshka, an underhanded and much-aspiring man, put unbounded trust in his own wealth and cleverness: and as to the miller, he would have risked his life for money.

And thus the struggle between the two parties began, fierce, yet courteous: they treated each other to shows of friendship, and conversed as before, and even went sometimes arm in arm to the tavern together.

The shrewder Lipka folk were soon aware that this coalition aimed at something more than the mere redress of KoziolвАЩs grievanceвБ†вАФpossibly at the VoytвАЩs office itself.

And the elders nodded their heads, saying:

вАЬOne man has made a good thing by the office: others may do so too!вАЭ

And as the days passed by, the village dissensions increased.

Once, about that time, there came round to every hut the news that there were Germans stopping at the tavern.

As someone guessed, they were no doubt bound for Podlesie.

The people were taken with a fit of uneasy curiosity. The news flew from orchard to orchard, it was discussed over the fences, and many a one hurried to the tavern to see.

It was true. Five large tilted wagons, each painted yellow and blue, and fitted with iron axletrees, stood outside the inn. They were laden with articles of furniture, and women were sitting inside. In the tavern bar, ten Germans were drinking.

They were tall, stout, bearded fellows, clad in dark-blue capotes, with silver chains that dangled from their bulging waists, and faces that literally shone with good feeding.

The peasants stood grouped together at a distance from them, calling for vodka, looking on and listening to their talk, but unable to make anything out. Matthew, who could speak Yiddish, tried speech with them by that means, and so fluently that the tavern-keeper looked at him in amazement. The Germans eyed him, but answered nothing. Gregory, the VoytвАЩs brother, then said a few German words to them: at which, grunting among themselves as swine do over a trough, they turned their backs on the peasants.

Matthew took fire at this. вАЬLetвАЩs punch their snouts for them!вАЭ he cried.

вАЬAye; or tickle their sides with a cudgel to make them speak.вАЭ

And Adam Klemba, a fiery young fellow, exclaimed:

вАЬIвАЩll hit this nearest one in the stomach; and if he strikes back, ye all come in for fair play.вАЭ

But they restrained him; and the Germans, probably guessing some harm was intended them, took with them a barrel of beer, and left the place.

вАЬHey, ye Long Trousers, do not hurry so: they might fall down by the way!вАЭ

And as they drove off, the peasants shouted after them:

вАЬBreed of swine!вАЭ

No sooner had they left than the Jew informed the peasants that the Germans had almost completed the purchase of Podlesie: fifteen families were to settle on that farm.

вАЬAnd then we shall be hemmed in and squeezed to death on our poor strips of land, while they stretch and multiply over those broad acres!вАЭ

вАЬThen let us bid higher and keep them out!вАЭ said Staho Ploshka to Gregory, who had spoken last. вАЬUse your wits, since ye think yourself clever!вАЭ

вАЬBlood of a dog!вАЭ Matthew shouted, thumping the bar with his fist; вАЬвАКвАЩtis a ruinous business, this! If they settle down in Podlesie, вАЩtwill be a hard thing for us to keep our homesteads in Lipka.вАЭ Of this he was sure: he had been about the world, and knew what Germans were.

His hearers were at first incredulous. All the same, it troubled them, and they fell a-thinking: how could evil come to them in Lipka from neighbours in Podlesie?

Every day, herdsmen and wayfarers came to tell how the lands of Podlesie were being measured, and landmarks laid down, and wells dug. Of this, too, so many as wandered out of curiosity in the direction of Vola had ocular demonstration.

But what was the true situation of affairs? That as yet they had not ascertained.

They urged the smith to find out, for he had made friends with the Germans and shod their horses; but he fought shy, and either learned or told them nothing.

It was Gregory who at last sought information and got at the truth.

The fact was that the Squire owed a certain German fifteen thousand roubles, which he could not pay. The latter proposed taking Podlesie for the debt, and paying the difference in ready money. The Squire seemed inclined to agree to this, but secretly looked out for some other purchaser, the German offering only sixty roubles an acre.

вАЬHe will have to agree,вАЭ Gregory declared. вАЬThe Manor is swarming with Jews, all crying out for their money. And the forester told me the Manor cows had been attached for the taxes. How, then, can he pay them? Everything is sold! And he may not cut down the forest, so long as his lawsuit with us is not ended.вБ†вАФNo, he must sell Podlesie at any price.вАЭ

вАЬWhy, such land is worth a hundred roubles an acre!вАЭ

вАЬThen buy it at that price, and he will be only too glad to sell!вАЭ

вАЬAlas, money is short! Whence is it to come?вАЭ

вАЬSo then, the Germans must get all, and we nothing!вАЭ

They talked on, with sad forebodings. How hard it was that such land should be lost to themвБ†вАФso near, so fruitful, so suitable for their sons and sons-in-law! They could have founded another village there, with fertile meadows and water in abundance.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But there, all was of no avail! The Germans were there, they would get the upper hand and crush the life out of the poor peasants.

вАЬAnd whither shall all these go?вАЭ the old men murmured sadly, looking on while their children played about the roads in the eveningвБ†вАФso numerous that the huts could scarce contain them. вАЬBut how is it possible to purchase any land, since we get barely enough to live on out of ours?вАЭ

They cudgelled their brains not a little; they even went to the priest for advice. But he could give them none. вАЬOut of an empty pot, nothing is ever got!вАЭ

вАЬAh! вАШmoney makes the mare goвАЩ; but, вАШwherever the poor man goes, the wind against him blows!вАЩвАКвАЭ

Useless complaints, useless lamentations!

To make matters worse, the weather grew excessively hot: July weather in May. The sun rose in the very East, a vast fire in the azure: on every height, in all sandy soil, the vegetables drooped and faded. The grass was sear and burnt upon the fallows; and the potato-plants, which had at first sprouted well, hardly covered the ground with their poor puny shoots. The autumn-sown lands were alone to suffer little; being already in the ear, they grew splendidly and very high: the low cottages they surrounded seemed to have shrunk yet lower, crouching earthwards with only their roofs well above the forest of waving corn.

The nights being so hot and sultry that it was painful to lie in the huts, people slept in the orchards.

As a consequence of the great heats, of those troubles which beset them in troops, and also of the hard times preceding the harvest (harder than usual that year), it came to pass that the people of Lipka quarrelled and fought among themselves more than ever. Everyone seemed to enjoy thwarting his neighbour, and life there became a real torment. From the peep of day, the village rang with bickerings and angry words, some new contest taking place every day. Now it was Kobus and his wife, who had such a set-to that the priest had to go and rebuke and reconcile them; now BalcerekвАЩs wife, who came to blows with Gulbas over a pig that had strayed into her carrot-patch; now Ploshkova, who had a row with the Soltys, on account of their goslingsвАЩ getting mixed: besides countless wrangles about the children, or unneighbourly behaviour, or anything that could serve for a squabble and outcries and noisy invectives. There seemed to be a curse on the village, bringing no end of altercations, breaches of the peace, and lawsuits.

Ambrose even made fun of this waspish disposition before strangers.

вАЬThis year times are not so very hard for me before harvest, by the goodness of Providence! No one dies, no one is born, no one is married; but they daily offer me vodka, and flatter me, and beg me to bear witness for them! Let them but wrangle so a few years more, and I shall have drunk myself to death!вАЭ

Lipka was in a bad way indeed; but things went worst of all at DominikovaвАЩs.

Simon had returned with the others, AndrewвАЩs leg was well now; they were not in sore straits as others were, and things ought to have gone on as of old. Far from it! Her sons would no longer obey her. They were grown rebellious, always at loggerheads with her, objected to being beaten, and refused to do womanвАЩs work!

вАЬYe must hire a maidservant,вАЭ they said tartly, вАЬor do the work yourself.вАЭ

Now Dominikova had ruled them with a rod of iron, and tyrannized over them for many a year; so she was very much shocked to see her own children rise up against her now.

вАЬGrant me patience!вАЭ she would scream on such occasions, flying in a rage, and taking up her stick; but they made stout resistance, and were as stubborn as she. Every day there were fearful quarrels, hunts all over the premises, and so on, till the neighbours would rush in to smooth matters over.

The priest himself called her sons to him, and exhorted them to live in concord and obedience. They heard him out with patient respect, kissed his hand and humbly embraced his knees as in duty bound; but they behaved as before.

вАЬWe are no children; we know what to do, and Mother must yield. Why, the whole village was making laughingstocks of us!вАЭ

Choler and exasperation had made the old dame yellow as a quince. Do what she might, she could not get the better of them; and now, instead of attending church services or gossiping as she had been accustomed, she had to work at home! She was always calling on Yagna to assist her; but from her too she got shame and sorrow in plenty.

The Voyt would look in frequently, to take her advice, he said: in reality, to get Yagna out with him, and play pranks with her in the garden.

Nothing can be hidden in a village: all knew perfectly well what was going on. And their guilty love becoming more and more openly scandalous, certain good people spoke several times to the old woman on the subject.

What was she to do? Yagna, notwithstanding prayers and supplications, flaunted her wantonness as though to spite her mother. The most grievous sin, the most shameful obloquy, was preferable in her eyes to staying by that husband whom she loathed.

Hanka too did nothing to oppose this state of things, and said so openly.

вАЬSo long as there is no one to prevent the Voyt from wasting the communityвАЩs money, she may do as she pleases. He grudges her naught, fetches her all he can from town, and would, if he could, put her in a frame of gold. Let them have their sportвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and see the end of it!вБ†вАФI have naught in common with them!вАЭ

And truly, she had troubles enough of her own. She had given the lawyer whatever he asked, but could as yet not tell when Antek was to be tried, nor what destiny awaited him. And meanwhile he was pining away in prison, hoping God would have mercy on him.вБ†вАФIn the cabin, besides, things went ill.

Pete had grown insolent of lateвБ†вАФno doubt the smith had tampered with him; he did no more work than he chose. Once she had been in town: he had spent the whole day out of doors; and as she threatened to call him to account before Antek on his return:

вАЬOn his return?вАЭ was the jeering reply. вАЬBandits are never set free in such wise!вАЭ

The impertinent words made her blood boil, and she would have longed to strike him on the mouth; but what good would that have done? She had to pocket her affronts, till the right time should come. Otherwise, the man would leave, and all would be thrown on her hands. She could hardly manage to pull through as it was, and her health was breaking down under the strain. вАЬSteel is devoured by rust, and rocks last but for a seasonвАЭ; how, then, could a feeble woman hold out forever?

One dayвБ†вАФMay was then drawing to an endвБ†вАФthe priest had driven off with the organist to a local festival. Ambrose had been drinking so deep with the Germans (who now looked frequently in at the tavern) that he was not there to toll the evening Angelus, nor to open the church-door for the May service!

It was therefore decided to have the service in the burying-ground, where, close to the lich-gate, there stood a little shrine containing a statue of the Mother of God. Every May, the girls adorned it with paper ribbons and a gilt crown, and cast wild flowers all around, doing likewise all they could to preserve it from complete ruin. It was of great antiquity, and so cracked and crumbling and dilapidated that the birds no longer made their nests in it; and if a shepherd-boy ever took shelter there, it was only during the autumn rains. To some extent it was screened from the winter storms by the churchyard trees, the old lindens, some slender birches, and a few crosses which stood near, leaning out of the perpendicular.

A good many people had assembled, and quickly decked the shrine with flowers and verdure; and, having placed a taper and some small lamps at the feet of the statue, they knelt down piously.

The blacksmith bent in front before the threshold, sprinkled with tulips and eglantine blossoms; and he struck up a hymn.

It was then much after sunset, and growing dark; but the sky was still burning and golden in the West, with pale emerald higher up. All was very still; the long locks of the birches streamed down in cascades, and the corn stooped down, as though listening to the shrill quavering of the crickets.

The herds were wending their way home; and from field and village and pathways now unseen, there floated the graziersвАЩ noisy songs, mingled with long melancholy lowings. And the people looked into their MotherвАЩs face, and lifted up their voices, while she stretched her hands out in benediction over the world.

вАЬGood night, O Lily white!
Good night!вАЭ

The scent of the young birch-trees filled the air, and the nightingales began to try their notes, first in broken bursts, then gathering force, and finally breaking out into foaming streams of golden euphonyвБ†вАФwonderful trills of long-drawn music, of melody dropping like pearls; while at no great distance resounded the violin of Mr.¬†Yacek, which accompanied the human singers so sweetly, so gently, and so powerfully at once, it seemed that it came from the rye-stalks rustling against each other, or that the soil itself were breathing that chant of May.

All then sang together in ecstasies: the people, the birds, the violin; and when they paused to take breath awhile, an innumerable choir of frogs raised their husky voices all in tune and whirringly, as if hurrying them on to begin again.

So the chant went onвБ†вАФnow these singers, now those.

The service lasted very long, and in the end the smith called out more than once to those behind him:

вАЬPray, do not drag the words so!вАЭ For many of them were lengthening out the notes beyond measure.

His remark to Matthias Klemba went even farther: вАЬDonвАЩt bellow so; youвАЩre not following the oxen!вАЭ And at last they sang together all in harmony, and their voices were like a flock of doves soaring up in the dusky sky.

вАЬGood night, O Lily white!
Good night!
Mary, our heartsвАЩ delight,
Good night!вАЭ

It was now black overhead, warm, and very quiet; but a few stars began to appear and sparkle and twinkle like dewdrops.

The girls in couples, arms about waists, sang together as they went home.

Hanka was returning alone, carrying her baby and absorbed in thought, when the smith approached and walked by her side.

She held her peace until close to her home; then, seeing him still beside her, she said:

вАЬAre ye coming in, Michael?вАЭ

вАЬOnly into the porch,вАЭ he answered in a low tone; вАЬI have to speak with you.вАЭ

She felt somewhat agitated. Had he any fresh misery in store for her?

вАЬYou have probably been to see Antek,вАЭ he said.

вАЬYes, but was not let in.вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАЩTis what I feared!вАЭ

вАЬSay, then, what ye know!вАЭ She was shaking with apprehension.

вАЬWhat I know? Only so much as I could get out of the police inspector.вАЭ

вАЬAnd what is that?вАЭ She pressed her little one closer.

вАЬThat Antek is not to be released until after the trial.вАЭ

вАЬHow is that?вАЭ she stammered hoarsely. вАЬThe lawyer told me the contrary.вАЭ

вАЬвАКвАЩTis because he would take to flight. In such cases, a prisoner is never set at liberty. Bear in mind that I come to you as a friend today.вБ†вАФBygones are bygones.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Though you will see one day that I was right.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But hear what I have to say now: I am speaking the truth as in the confessional.вБ†вАФAntek is in a very sorry plight, and sure to be cruelly punished: perchance for ten years! You hear?вАЭ

вАЬI do, and believe not one word,вАЭ she said, with sudden self-control.

вАЬSeeing is believing; and I have told you the truth.вАЭ

вАЬAfter your fashion,вАЭ she replied, smiling ironically.

He seemed offended, and assured her warmly that he had come for no other purpose but to give her friendly advice. She listened as he talked, looking round impatiently; the cows lowed unmilked in their byres, the geese were still out of doors, the colt and Lapa sporting in the enclosure, and the little ones playing in the barn: while of all he was telling her she believed nothing. вАЬBut IвАЩll let him talk on,вАЭ she thought, вАЬtill I find out what heвАЩs aiming atвАЭ; and she held herself on the alert.

вАЬWhat is to be done?вАЭ she asked mechanically.

вАЬThere is something,вАЭ he whispered.

She faced him suddenly.

вАЬPay down enough money, and he will be released even before the trial. And then he can escape. Even to вАШHamericaвАЩ! They will not catch him there.вАЭ

вАЬLord! to вАШHamericaвАЩ!вАЭ she cried, appalled.

вАЬHush! I tell you in great secrecy. вАЩTis what the Squire said to me. вАШLet him get away,вАЩ he said; вАШten years of Siberia will ruin any manвАЩs life.вАЩвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That he told me but yesterday.вАЭ

вАЬWhat! and flee from our villageвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ our childrenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ our lands?вАЭ It was the only misfortune that appealed to her.

вАЬPay the sum wanted, and Antek will do all the rest.вБ†вАФGive it to them.вАЭ

вАЬBut whereвБ†вАФwhere am I to get it?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ O my God!вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Away to the very back of the world.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ So far from everyone!вАЭ

вАЬThey ask for five hundred roubles. Why, ye have father-in-lawвАЩs money: take it and pay; we shall reckon together laterвБ†вАФonly save Antek!вАЭ

She saw the trap, and started to her feet.

вАЬObstinate dog! Always on the same trail!вАЭ she said, about to leave him.

вАЬThis is foolishness!вАЭ he exclaimed, losing patience. вАЬвАКвАЩTwas but a word that escaped me. Must ye take offence at a word, when your man is rotting in jail?вБ†вАФOh, he shall know how much ye do to set him free!вАЭ

She sat down again, quite bewildered.

He talked to her for some time about вАЬHamerica,вАЭ and folk he knew that had gone there, who wrote letters home, and even sent their families money. Antek might get away at once; Michael knew a Jew who had wafted many a one over the frontier. Multitudes got away thus. Hanka, too, might follow later, without attracting attention. Gregory would return from the army, and repay all from the inheritance; or if he could not, a purchaser would readily be found.

вАЬTake ye counsel of the priest,вАЭ he concluded; вАЬhe will approve my plan, as ye will see. I have only urged you to do right, and for no profit of my own.вБ†вАФBut breathe not a word of this to anyone else, or the gendarmes will know of it. And then he would not get out of prison at all, and be perchance put in irons into the bargain,вАЭ he concluded grimly.

вАЬBut where is the money to come from that will set him free?вАЭ she said, with a groan.

вАЬI know a man in Modlitsa who would lend itвБ†вАФat high interest. Oh, the money can be found! IвАЩll lay my life I could manage that!вАЭ

For a long while he went on counselling her, and at last slipped away abruptly. She, lost in thought, took no note of his going.

Everybody else had gone to bed, except Vitek, who seemed to be waiting for his mistress. The moon went up through the heavens, a silver reaping-hook moving athwart the deep expanse. Over the meadows crept white sheets of mist; above the rye was hanging the dust of its saffron pollen; moveless as an ice-field, the millpond glittered amongst the trees. Broken by the nightingalesвАЩ rills of bubbling music, the silence made the ears ring and tingle.

вАЬHeavens! To flee oneвАЩs village, and oneвАЩs landвБ†вАФand all!вАЭ she thought, the same idea ever returning; and her dread grew more intense, and she felt her trembling heart shrink in the clutch of pain.

Lapa then howled aloud; the birds sang no more, and the gales whistled and moaned among the dark shadows of the branches.

вАЬLapa now sees KubaвАЩs ghost!вАЭ Vitek muttered, and crossed himself with awe.

вАЬFoolish one! go to bed!вАЭ said Hanka.

вАЬBut indeed he does come, and looks after the horses, and brings them their fodder. Aye, and not once only!вАЭ

She took no notice of his words. All was now plunged in deep stillness; and there she sat, repeating, as one paralysed with appalling anguish: вАЬTo flee a whole world away!вБ†вАФAnd forever, merciful Jesus!вБ†вАФForever!вАЭ
